planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; farkleberry is a native deciduous to semi-evergreen blueberry relative — the largest and most drought-tolerant Vaccinium in the Southeast — producing small, dark-blue to black, edible but astringent berries that are best dried or used in cooking rather than eaten fresh.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in full sun to part shade; drought-tolerant once established, requiring no irrigation in the Southeast. Vaccinium arboreum (farkleberry or sparkleberry) is the largest native Vaccinium in the southeastern United States — a multi-stemmed, deciduous to semi-evergreen large shrub or small tree reaching 6–15 feet with smooth, reddish-brown exfoliating bark that is ornamentally attractive year-round. It is native to the dry, acidic soils of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, piedmont, and lower Appalachians, tolerating drought, sandy soils, and poor fertility far better than cultivated highbush blueberries. The small (1/4 inch), dark blue-black berries are technically edible, but are mealy, seedy, and quite astringent when eaten fresh — quite different from cultivated blueberries. They are far better suited to drying (they were used extensively by southeastern Native Americans as a dried fruit), cooking into jams and pies, or fermenting. Birds and wildlife consume the abundant fruit crops enthusiastically. As a garden plant, farkleberry is an underused native with beautiful four-season interest: white urn-shaped flowers in spring, glossy foliage in summer, brilliant red-orange fall color, and attractive bark in winter.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Vaccinium arboreum
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Blueberries

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; goumi berry is an East Asian nitrogen-fixing shrub producing elongated, cherry-red berries with silver speckles — sweet-tart and lycopene-rich — that ripen in late spring to early summer, several weeks ahead of most other berries; the roots fix nitrogen through Frankia bacterial symbiosis, improving soil fertility over time.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; extremely adaptable — drought-tolerant, salt-tolerant, and fixes nitrogen. Elaeagnus multiflora (goumi berry or natsugumi) is a deciduous shrub from China and Japan producing one of the earliest ripening berry crops in the edible landscape — the elongated, cherry-red, silver-speckled fruits ripen in late May to June (in zone 6–7), several weeks before blueberries or raspberries, filling a gap in the fresh fruit calendar. The fruits are tart-sweet, somewhat astringent when slightly underripe, with a pleasant fresh berry flavor when fully red; they are rich in lycopene (higher concentration than tomatoes), vitamins A, C, and E, and flavonoids. Unlike its aggressive invasive relative autumn olive (E. umbellata), goumi is not considered invasive in most regions — it produces less seed, spreads less aggressively, and is widely grown in permaculture and food forest systems. The roots fix atmospheric nitrogen through Frankia actinorrhizal symbiosis, making goumi a valuable soil-building companion plant in the food forest understory layer. The attractive silvery-green foliage and small, fragrant, creamy-yellow flowers in spring are also ornamentally pleasing.
sources: University of Maine Cooperative Extension — Goumi Berry

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; safou or African pear is one of the most nutritionally important indigenous fruit trees of Central and West Africa, producing clusters of deep purple-blue, olive-shaped fruits rich in essential fatty acids and protein — grilled or boiled, the flesh softens to a buttery, avocado-like consistency and is a dietary staple in Cameroon, Nigeria, DRC, and across the humid tropical forest zone.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; young trees benefit from partial shade and consistent moisture. Dacryodes edulis (safou, African pear, or bush butter tree) is one of sub-Saharan Africa's most culturally and nutritionally important indigenous food trees, native to the humid tropical forests of West and Central Africa — particularly Cameroon, Nigeria, Gabon, and the Democratic Republic of Congo. The fruits are produced in large pendant clusters directly on the branches: deep purple-blue to violet, oval-oblong, 2–4 inches long, resembling a fat olive. The fruit is not eaten raw — the hard, firm flesh must be boiled or flame-roasted to soften, at which point it transforms into a rich, buttery, smooth, avocado-like consistency with a unique, savory-nutty flavor profile. The fruit pulp contains 35–55% oil with a composition similar to olive oil and palm oil combined, making safou one of the most calorie-dense and nutritionally complete forest fruits in Africa. It is also a significant protein source compared to most fruits. In its native range, safou is widely eaten alongside corn, cassava, and plantain as a major dietary fat source, and is sold extensively in West African urban markets. Outside Africa, safou is almost entirely unknown but is gaining interest as a potential food security crop and agroforestry tree in tropical regions worldwide.
sources: FAO — Dacryodes edulis: a Multipurpose African Tree
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — Dacryodes edulis
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; wild medlar is a tough, drought-resistant shrub or small tree from the dry bushveld of southern and eastern Africa — producing round, yellow-brown, sweet-acid fruits eaten fresh, dried, or fermented into traditional beer across the region from South Africa to Kenya.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; exceptionally drought-tolerant once established — a true bushveld species. Vangueria infausta (wild medlar or African medlar) is a deciduous shrub or small tree from the dry savanna and bushveld regions of eastern and southern Africa — common across South Africa, Zimbabwe, Botswana, Mozambique, and Kenya in seasonally dry habitats. The plant produces round to slightly flattened, 1–2 inch fruits that ripen from green to yellowish-brown, with soft, sweet-acid, mealy flesh and 4–5 large seeds. The flavor when fully ripe is pleasant — sweet with fruity-acid notes somewhat reminiscent of a tart apple-medlar — and the fruits are eaten fresh throughout rural sub-Saharan Africa, as well as dried into a concentrated fruit leather, fermented into traditional beer, or made into jams and sauces. The tree is highly drought-resistant, adapted to seasonally dry and poor soils of the African bushveld, making it a candidate for water-wise agroforestry in dry subtropical climates. The leaves, bark, and roots have extensive use in southern African traditional medicine. As an ornamental and food plant in zone 9–11 dry climates, wild medlar is a low-maintenance, wildlife-friendly tree that produces an edible crop under difficult conditions.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Vangueria infausta
Plants for a Future — Vangueria infausta
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — African Indigenous Fruits

common_name: Natal Orange (Kaffir Orange, Spiny Monkey Orange)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Natal orange is a thorny deciduous tree from sub-Saharan Africa producing hard-shelled, yellow-green fruits resembling small oranges — the sweet-acid, jelly-like pulp is edible (unlike the toxically famous S. nux-vomica); the hard shell is used traditionally for containers and decorative gourds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; very drought-tolerant once established; the thorny nature deters browsing animals. Strychnos spinosa (Natal orange, spiny monkey orange, or kaffir orange) is a thorny deciduous tree from sub-Saharan Africa — native to the savanna, bushveld, and dry woodland regions of South Africa, Zimbabwe, Mozambique, and eastward to Kenya and Tanzania. The tree bears the most remarkable fruits in the genus: large (3–4 inch diameter), perfectly round, hard-shelled, bright yellow-green when ripe, strongly resembling oranges externally despite being completely unrelated (in the Strychnaceae family). IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE: Unlike Strychnos nux-vomica (the strychnine tree, which has highly toxic seeds), S. spinosa fruit pulp is edible — the sweet-acid, jelly-like, cream to yellow flesh surrounding the large seeds inside has a pleasant, fruity, somewhat date-like flavor and is widely eaten in rural southern Africa. The seeds are bitter and should be avoided. The extremely hard shell of the dried fruit has been used for millennia as a container, bowl, and decorated gourd throughout sub-Saharan Africa. The roots, bark, and leaves have extensive traditional medicinal applications in southern African herbalism. A remarkably tough, drought-resistant tree for dry subtropical collectors' gardens in zone 9–11.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Strychnos spinosa
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — African Indigenous Fruits

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; rambai is a Southeast Asian cauliflorous fruit tree — the fruits hang in long pendant clusters directly from the trunk and main branches — dioecious (separate male and female trees required for fruit), best suited to zone 11–12 humid tropics.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in part shade; plant at least one male and one female tree for fruit production — rambai is dioecious. Baccaurea motleyana (rambai or rambeh) is a tropical Southeast Asian fruit tree native to the humid lowland forests of the Malay Peninsula, Thailand, and Borneo — closely related to langsat (Lansium domesticum) in lifestyle and fruit appearance, though in a different botanical family (Phyllanthaceae). The tree produces long, pendulous clusters of small (1–1.5 inch), thin-skinned, yellow to pale-orange fruits directly on the trunk and main branches (cauliflory), similar in habit to cacao and jackfruit. The sweet-acid, translucent-white, jelly-like flesh has a pleasant, mild, tropical flavor — somewhat sweet, gently tart, with a slight astringency when slightly underripe. Rambai is eaten fresh in Malaysia and southern Thailand, and is also used in traditional cooking and beverages. The tree requires genuinely hot, humid, tropical conditions without frost — suitable only for zone 11–12 in the continental US (extreme South Florida, Hawaii). It is dioecious, requiring both a male and female tree for fruit production; grafted female trees are occasionally available from specialty tropical nurseries. Almost entirely unknown outside Southeast Asia in cultivation; an exceptional and very rare find for the dedicated tropical fruit collector.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Baccaurea species
Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI)
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
